comment_content: It teach the basic knowledge of the computer seciruty and I think it is really funny and Prof. Ding is a nice old man.
comment_teaching: I like the teaching style of Prof. Ding. He is very kind and helpful for students.
comment_workload: The workload is low and I don't think student need to spend a lot of time on the work. I enjoy going to the class and have the nice time with Prof. Ding.
comment_grading: The Grade is good and I got B+. Most of people could get an upper B grade in the class. The attendance you just need to have 12 times logged in during the whole semster.

comment_content:
The course is structured into classical cryptography (about 2/3) and cyber security (about 1/3).

The course syllabus consists of the following topics:
• History of cryptography
• Design and analysis of one-key ciphers
• Public-key cryptography
• Partially homomorphic encryption
• Hash functions, keyed hash functions
• Digital signature
• Data origin authentication
• Data integrity
• Nonrepudiation
• Anonymity on the Internet
• Secret sharing
• Key management and public key infrastructure
• Cryptographic protocols
• Email security
• Web security
• Network security
• Distributed systems security
• Firewalls
• Virtual private networks.  

The reason for my bad content grading is that the course content seems outdated and a lot of interesting more recent topics are completely missing, for example:
• Fully homomorphic encryption
• Zero-knowledge proofs (e.g. SNARKs)
• Multi-party computation
comment_teaching: Instructor didn't seem to have a lot of excitement which might be as a result of teaching the course for so many years. He basically reads out the lecture slides. However, he is happy to answer any questions patiently.
comment_workload:
There were 4 assignments in total, each can be solved in several hours. Some assignments require a good foundation in number theory, which you are required to learn by yourself if you are not familiar with it.
For the exam, a good understanding of the course material was necessary, but the final exam was open book so the workload for final exam preparation was low.
comment_grading:
• Lecture attendance 6%
• Four assignments 44%
• Final exam 50%

Grading is fairly generous. I would consider this course to be an "easy A" course.

The final exam was open book, i.e. you were allowed to bring a laptop in offline mode so you could use any downloaded or paper documents.

comment_content: The contents provide good introduction to basic security concepts and standard (but quite old) cryptographic primitives, as well as some antiquated real world security systems like PGP and IPSec. Introduction to more state of the art security and cryptography research such as ZKP and post-quantum primitives would have been appreciated.
comment_teaching: Lectures are good, with some minor interactions with students in class. Lectures are easy to understand, but deeper mathematical concepts are skipped over in favor of higher level presentation of security systems.
comment_workload: Attend lecture. Spend about 30 minutes per week on assignments. Study a little for final exam. This is an easy course.
comment_grading:
Assignments - Easy to get full score
Attendance - Just attend most classes
Final Exam - Easy final exam, mostly non-mathematical problems. Exam is open book, and computer notes are allowed (no internet - wifi must be turned off)

comment_content:
First half is on cryptographic primitives (symmetric key cipher, assymetric key cipher (RSA), Digital Signature Scheme (DSS), secret sharing scheme, etc). Second half is on real-world security system (IPSec, IKEv2, Keyberos, SSL, SSH, etc).

Surprisingly, there is no formal treatment to security.

I personally dislike the second part and would rather want it to be on some modern crytographic tools like zero-knowledge proof, blockchain, etc...
comment_teaching: The professor is experienced and nice but his intonation is not the best among CSE. The professor mentioned that he tried to make the course less technical this semester. I am not sure what changed, but it's still somewhat technical, not in the sense that the mathamatics is difficult, but the fact that the lecture talks about some intricate details of some protocols (e.g., which protocol must support AES, which protocol do not require both sides to support any cipher, which protocol needs an alert protocol, and which modes does a protocol have, etc......). Those are actually not that interesting for beginners.
comment_workload: The workload is very tiny. Only 4 assignments which are quite easy. However, I actually hoped that there are more assignments given so that I can understand the protocols in real world security system better, especially on IPSec, which is quite technical.
comment_grading: The grading is promised to be nice. The final examination is not difficult. I personally think that this course is a popular choice for PG only because it's an easy course and the grading is nice...

comment_content:
First half: Cryptography

1. One key (symmetric) cipher
1.1 One-key block cipher
1.2 Design Idea
1.3 AES
1.4 Stream Cipher
1.5 Key Management

2. Public key cipher
2.1 Introduction
2.2 RSA and ElGamal
2.3 PKI
2.4 Homomorphic Encryption

3. Hashing
3.1 Keyed hash function
3.2 SHA

4. Security Protocols
5. DSS
6. Secret Sharing

Second half: Network Security

7. Application layer
7.1 PGP
7.2 Kerberos

8. Transport layer
8.1 SSL
8.2 SSH

9. Network Layer
9.1 IPSec, AH, ESP
9.2 IKEv2

10. Other
10.1 VPN
10.2 Firewall
10.3 Anonymity (Onion routing, etc.)

I like the first half. The design of lecture notes is very clean and easy to follow. Some contents are insightful, especially the lecture about homomorphic encryption. To understand the content of this part, it suffices to know about the number theory part in COMP2711 (plus a little bit of quadratic residue and discrete indices) and some basic concepts in abstract algebra, such as the Galois field (which is kind of an abstraction of modular arithmetic).

I don't quite like the second part, however. The lecture slides contain many subtle details about each protocol, but explanation is not so detailed, and sometimes even lacking. You need to hear the professor's words carefully in order to understand. The contents did not provide much insight, but just some plain details, unfortunately. If there is more focus on attacking these protocols, like COMP3632, then it might be better.
comment_teaching: Although very detailed, the teaching pace is very slow, and the tone makes the lecture so boring. After attending 10 lectures, I cannot bear anymore and skip all other lectures.
comment_workload:
3 Assignments
Don't know how to do? Google or find source

1 Project
May need 1 to 2 days to finish

1 Final
Not much to prepare. You can even prepare the exam during the exam time, if you want :)   I actually only watch the relevant part of lecture video not until the 4-hour exam.

Btw. Professor always emphasizes that this course is tough and many maths are involved. Don't be scared by this statement. Even an undergraduate student like me can handle this course quite handily.
comment_grading:
3 Assignments: 45%
Project: 20%
Final Exam: 35%

The three assignments basically ask you some simple proofs (or attack) and some concepts which can be subtle or tricky. The proofs and attacks are similar to proofs in number theory, so if you are comfortable with number theory, then they are kind of trivial. However, you might need sources and Google to answer the conceptual questions. Assignment 3 did not have source and IMO is the trickiest one.

Project basically asks you to compare public key cryptography with identity-based encryption. You don't really need to understand the technical details to write a good report, although it is interesting to dive deeper. Technical details might involve some number theory which is a liitle bit more advanced than those in the lecture (e.g. Jacobi and Legendre symbol), and also ellpitic curve cryptography.

Final Exam consists of only 9 MCs in 4 hours, but they are quite tricky. Some MC actually requires you to do proof, but it is still all-or-nothing because if you got the proof wrong in the halfway, then you got the wrong choice anyway. Other conceptual/factual questions are tricky in the way that you have to read the statement and comprehend the concept very carefully. Even though there is sufficient time for you to think carefully and repeatedly, it is still difficult to get a correct choice.

The grading scheme is really good. Basically everyone could obtain at least 90% in assignments and project, so the final mainly determines your grade. Even if you got only 1 answer correct, you could still get a B. Yes, B seems to be the lowest grade you can get other than F. Roughly speaking, the median grade is A-, and it can be obtained by answering 5 questions correctly. However, gettting A and A+ are difficult, and you need to answer at least 7-8 questions correctly in order to obtain it.

comment_content:

Basic idea of Cryptography, including
RSA

SHA-1
